figures.caption: Figure 4 Cumulative distribution of test pass rates across models on ProgramBench.

Figure 5 Per-task pass rates for the 40 most-starred repositories in ProgramBench, grouped by reference language and sorted by average model score within each group. Each cell shows one model’s test pass rate on one task.

Table 2 Main results on ProgramBench. % Resolved is the primary metric: the fraction of 200 tasks where all tests pass. % Almost relaxes this to instances with ≥95% of tests passing. We also report average API calls and cost per task.
Table 12 Complete list of repositories in ProgramBench with brief descriptions and difficulty scores. † Networking tool; tested over localhost (see §A.2.4).

Table 9 Distribution of ProgramBench task instances across functional categories. Each repository is assigned to a category based on its primary utility. Representative examples showcase the diversity within each category.
Table 4 Line coverage (%) for generated vs. native behavioral suites across 12 repositories that maintain an identifiable integration or end-to-end test suite, sorted by ∆.
Figure 6 Effect of requiring a different implementation language. Filled circles show default scores; open circles show scores under the constraint.
Figure 7 Line coverage of our generated test suites versus project size (source lines excluding dependencies) for a sample of 100 tasks.
Figure 22 Distribution of GitHub community and development history metrics across ProgramBench repositories: stars, contributors, commits, and repository age.
Figure 28 Implementation language chosen by each model, as a percentage of runs. Models are free to choose any language; preferences vary significantly across models.
Figure 26 Cumulative distribution of trajectory lengths (API calls) per model. Lines that reach 100% before the 1,000-step limit indicate that all runs for that model submitted voluntarily.
Figure 12 Percentage of the final codebase produced by the single largest edit, per model. For instance, GPT 5.4 writes a median of 96% of its code in one turn.

Figure 24 Distribution of per-task test pass rates by reference language across all models. Each box spans the interquartile range with the median shown in white; individual task scores are overlaid as jittered points. Two tasks whose reference language is the sole representative of its family (one Haskell, one Java) are omitted.
Figure 25 Per-instance test pass rate vs. API calls (left) and cost (right). Each point is one (task, model) run. The x-axes are clipped at the 95th percentile for readability.
Figure 30 LoC ratio by reference language for solutions passing ≥75% of tests. C/C++ references see the largest gap, while Rust references are closest to parity.
Figure 29 LoC ratio (model / reference) per model for solutions passing ≥75% of tests. Dashed line marks parity.

Figure 15 Example of a 5–4 judge disagreement. Claude Sonnet 4.6 read dependency source code from the local Cargo registry cache while working on the handlr task. Five judges flagged this as source code lookup (red); four called it legitimate, noting the files are third-party libraries, not the project itself (green).

Figure 16 Distribution of judge votes per task for each model evaluated with internet access. Each bar shows how many tasks received exactly k cheating flags from the 9-judge panel. Tasks at k ≥ 5 are flagged by majority vote. Claude Sonnet 4.6 shows a bimodal pattern (many tasks at 0 and 9), while Claude Opus 4.6 has more mass in the ambiguous middle range, explaining its higher disagreement rate.
Table 7 The six specialized configs used in the decomposed test generation strategy. Each config prompts a SWE-agent to write tests targeting a narrow behavioral category of the executable.
Figure 19 Distribution of total/code lines per repository.
Figure 18 Distribution of total/code files per repository.

Figure 21 Distribution of runtime dependencies across ProgramBench repositories. Counts are extracted by parsing root-level package manifests (Cargo.toml, go.mod, CMakeLists.txt, etc.), excluding development and test dependencies.

Figure 8 Confusion matrix of reference vs. model language. Each cell shows the percentage (and count) of runs per reference language. Models generally prefer, in descending order, Python, Go, Rust, Shell, C/C++. Model-specific breakdowns are visualized in Figure 28.
Figure 9 Reference vs. model code lines on a log–log scale. The y=x diagonal indicates parity. Only solutions passing ≥75% of tests are shown.
Figure 31 Test pass rate vs. model code lines across all 1,800 runs. Points are colored by model.
Figure 23 Distribution of test pass rates across all 1,800 leaderboard runs in 5% bins. The median pass rate across all runs is 32%.
Table 8 Assertion lint rules. HIGH rules indicate tests likely to pass trivially incorrect implementations. MED rules indicate weak but not necessarily vacuous assertions.

Figure 17 Distribution of test volume and source across all ProgramBench task instances. Left: each dot is one task; the x-axis shows the total number of tests (log scale) and the y-axis shows the percentage of tests harvested from existing repository test suites versus self-generated tests. Bin counts are annotated above the plot. Right: global split of harvested vs. self-generated tests across all tasks.

Figure 10 Distribution of action types across agent turns for four representative models. Each bar shows the total number of actions of each type at a given turn index, aggregated across all task instances. The natural decay in bar height reflects trajectories of varying length ending at different points.

Table 11 Macro-averaged test pass rate by difficulty bin: for each task instance we compute the fraction of tests passed, then average these fractions within each bin. Rates decrease monotonically from Easy to Hard for every model.
